en.extract:
The Indian cuckooshrike (Coracina macei) is a species of bird in the cuckooshrike family Campephagidae that is found on the Indian subcontinent. Formerly under the English name "large cuckooshrike" this species included many subspecies and had a large range that included Southeast Asia.


Taxonomy
Two subspecies are recognised

C. m. macei (Lesson, RP, 1831) – central, south India
C. m. layardi (Blyth, 1866) – Sri Lanka
The Indian cuckooshrike formerly included additional subspecies (under the English name "large cuckooshrike"):

Oriental cuckooshrike now includes 5 former subspecies of the large cuckooshrike.
Malayan cuckooshrike was formerly a subspecies of the large cuckooshrike.


Description

Adult males have a broad and well-marked eye stripe which is pale in females. The throat and breast are grey in males and the abdomen and flanks are finely barred. Females have the throat and breast also with barring which extends further down and lacks the prominent whitish vent of the male. They are mostly insectivorous but also feed on figs and forest fruits and usually fly in small groups with a bounding flight just above the forest canopy. The Indian population has a loud call klu-eep and the birds have a characteristic habit of flicking their closed wings one after the other upon landing on a perch. The same wing movements are also used during courtship.


Breeding
The species breeds in the dry months of winter. The nest is a shallow saucer placed in the fork of a horizontal branch at some height above the ground. The saucer is made of twigs and grass decorated on the outside with cobwebs and with little lining. The typical clutch is three eggs in peninsular India and two around Bengal.


References

The '''Indian cuckooshrike''' ('''''Coracina macei''''') is a species of bird in the cuckooshrike family [[Campephagidae]] that is found on the [[Indian subcontinent]]. Formerly under the English name "large cuckooshrike" this species included many subspecies and had a large range that included Southeast Asia.

== Taxonomy==
Two [[subspecies]] are recognised<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=August 2024 | title=Bristlehead, butcherbirds, woodswallows, Mottled Berryhunter, ioras, cuckooshrikes | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/butcherbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=3 October 2024 }}</ref>
* ''C. m. macei'' ([[René Lesson|Lesson, RP]], 1831) – central, south India
* ''C. m. layardi'' ([[Edward Blyth|Blyth]], 1866) – Sri Lanka

The Indian cuckooshrike formerly included additional subspecies (under the English name "large cuckooshrike"):<ref name=ioc/>
* [[Oriental cuckooshrike]] now includes 5 former subspecies of the large cuckooshrike.
* [[Malayan cuckooshrike]] was formerly a subspecies of the large cuckooshrike.

==Description ==
[[File:Large_Cuckooshrike_(Coracina_macei).jpg|thumb|upright|right|A male from West Bengal]]
[[File:Indian cuckooshrike or Large cuckooshrike.jpg|thumb|Indian cuckooshrike or Large cuckooshrike]]
Adult males have a broad and well-marked eye stripe which is pale in females. The throat and breast are grey in males and the abdomen and flanks are finely barred. Females have the throat and breast also with barring which extends further down and lacks the prominent whitish vent of the male. They are mostly [[insectivorous]] but also feed on figs and forest fruits and usually fly in small groups with a bounding flight just above the forest canopy. The Indian population has a loud call ''klu-eep'' and the birds have a characteristic habit of flicking their closed wings one after the other upon landing on a perch. The same wing movements are also used during courtship.<ref name=hbk />

== Breeding ==
The species breeds in the dry months of winter. The nest is a shallow saucer placed in the fork of a horizontal branch at some height above the ground. The saucer is made of twigs and grass decorated on the outside with cobwebs and with little lining. The typical clutch is three eggs in peninsular India and two around Bengal.<ref name=hbk>{{Cite book| pages=14–18|title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan. Volume 6. Cuckoo-shrikes to Babaxes| edition=2| year=1996| place= Delhi| publisher= Oxford University Press| author1=Ali, Salim| author2=Ripley, S. Dillon}}</ref>

zh.extract:
大鹃鵙（学名：Coracina macei），又名花翅山椒鸟，是一種分布於印度次大陸的鵑鵙，根據不同的分類方法，有時也涵蓋東南亞。這個物種經歷了漫長且多變的分類歷史，與東南亞地區的其他形態有密切關聯，一些作者將牠稱為印度鵑鵙，該名稱指的是包含印度半島的C. m. macei和斯里蘭卡的C. m. layardi的物種。不同來源對於該物種及其亞種的分類存在很大的分歧，目前尚未有明確的結論。


描述
  
成年雄鳥有明顯的寬眼紋，而雌鳥的眼紋較淡。雄鳥的喉部和胸部呈灰色，腹部和兩側有細密的條紋。雌鳥的喉部和胸部也有條紋，且條紋延伸得更低，缺乏雄鳥明顯的白色腹部。牠們主要是食蟲動物，但也會食用無花果和森林中的水果，通常以小群飛行，飛行高度略高於森林樹冠，飛行方式為波浪狀。印度的群體發出響亮的klu-eep叫聲，並有一個特有的習性，即在降落在棲枝上時會依次輕彈閉合的雙翼。這種翅膀動作也會用於求偶行為。


分類與系統學

認可的亞種有幾個，但該群體的分類存在很大的混亂，尚未完全解決。 國際鳥類學委員會（IOC）的分類認為以下亞種屬於macei物種：  

C. m. nipalensis（Hodgson，1836），分布於喜馬拉雅山脈（一些作者將其視為C. javensis的亞種）
C. m. macei（Lesson, R，1831），分布於印度半島
C. m. layardi（Blyth，1866），分布於斯里蘭卡
C. m. andamana（Neumann，1915），分布於安達曼群島
C. m. rexpineti（Swinhoe，1863），分布於中國東南部、台灣、老撾和越南
C. m. larvivora（Swinhoe，1910），分布於海南島（中國近海）
C. m. siamensis（Baker，1918），分布於緬甸和中國南部至中南半島南部
C. m. larutensis（Sharpe，1887），分布於馬來半島（一些作者將其視為C. javensis的亞種或獨立物種）
該物種曾一度被歸為黑臉鵑鵙（Coracina novaehollandiae）的亞種，隸屬於caledonica超種群內。


繁殖
該物種在乾燥的冬季進行繁殖。巢是一個淺碟狀的結構，放置在距地面一定高度的水平樹枝的叉枝上。巢由樹枝和草構成，外部裝飾有蜘蛛網，內部幾乎沒有襯墊。典型的巢卵數在印度半島為三顆，在孟加拉附近則為兩顆。


参考文献
BirdLife International. Coracina macei. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2009.   [2010-10-26] （英语）. 
 花翅山椒 Coracina macei Lesson, 1830. 台湾物种名录. 台湾: 中央研究院生物多样性研究中心.   [2013-01-24] （中文（台湾））.
